1: The transition marker wayĕhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr opens Jeremiah's first major oracle (ch2-3). The oracle is structured as a rîḇ (covenant lawsuit) — a legal genre in which YHWH brings charges against Israel using the form of a legal proceeding: summons (v4), charges (v5-13), evidence (v14-30), verdict (v31-37). The prophet is the prosecuting witness.
2: Zākartî lāk ḥesed nĕʿûrāyik ʾahăḇat kĕlûlōtāyik — I remember the ḥesed (covenant-loyalty/devoted-love) of your youth, the love (ʾahăḇat) of your bridal days. Ḥesed is the covenantal term for the steadfast love that binds covenant partners; applying it to Israel rather than YHWH is striking — YHWH remembers Israel's early responsiveness as her own ḥesed toward him. Lektĕk ʾaḥărây bammidbar — your following me through the wilderness: the wilderness period idealized as the honeymoon of the covenant relationship (contra the Golden Calf incident — this is pastoral idealization, not historical).
3: Qōdeš Yiśrāʾēl lĕYHWH rēʾšît tĕḇûʾātô — Israel was holy to YHWH, the firstfruits of his harvest. Bikkûrîm (firstfruits) are the first and best portion consecrated to YHWH (Exod 23:19); applying the term to Israel as a nation inverts the harvest metaphor — Israel is what YHWH has "first-fruited" from the nations, hence sacred property; anyone who consumes/threatens the firstfruits incurs guilt.
4: The summons formula šimʿû dĕḇar-YHWH bêt Yaʿăqōḇ (Hear the word of YHWH, O house of Jacob) addresses the covenant assembly. Using Yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) alongside mišpeḥôt bêt Yiśrāʾēl (families of the house of Israel) recalls the patriarchal covenant identity — the people addressed are the heirs of the covenant promises.
5: Mah-māṣĕʾû ʾăḇôtêkem bî ʿāwel — what fault did your fathers find in me? The legal rîḇ begins with a challenge-question (rhetorical indictment): the defendant (YHWH) turns the accusation around. Wayyēlkû ʾaḥărê hahheḇel wayyehbāl — they went after the worthless thing and became worthless themselves. Heḇel is the Ecclesiastes word for vapor/breath/vanity; the verb wayyehbāl (they became heḇel) captures the principle that worshippers take on the character of their object of worship — those who worship empty things become empty.
6: The indictment exposes selective amnesia: the ancestors did not ask ʾayyēh YHWH hammĕʿăleh ʾōtānû mēʾereṣ Miṣrāyim — where is YHWH who brought us up from Egypt? The Exodus is the foundational saving event that should have generated ongoing remembrance and inquiry. The wilderness descriptions that follow — ʾereṣ ʿărāḇâ (land of the desert), ṣalmāwet (deep darkness, lit. shadow of death), lōʾ-ʿāḇar bāh ʾîš (no one crosses it) — emphasize that YHWH led them through impossible terrain, which makes the forgetting all the more inexcusable.
7: Waʾāḇîʾ ʾetkhem ʾel-ʾereṣ hakkarmel — I brought you into the land of the fruitful field (karmel = garden-land, orchard). The contrast is stark: from the uninhabitable wilderness of v6 to the productive garden-land. Watĕṭammĕʾû ʾet-ʾarṣî — you defiled my land: ṭāmēʾ (Piel intensive, make unclean) is the cultic term for ritual defilement; what is designated YHWH's inheritance (naḥălātî) has been made unclean — a sacrilege against covenantal property.
8: Four leadership groups charged: the priests who did not ask ʾayyēh YHWH; the tōpĕśê hattôrâ (Torah-handlers/lawyers) who did not know YHWH — the construction is ironic: those whose vocation is to know the Torah did not know the God the Torah reveals; the rulers (rōʿîm, shepherds) who transgressed; and the prophets who prophesied by Baal. The systemic failure runs from priest to prophet — every institutional mediator has failed.
9: Lākēn ʾōd ʾāriḇ ʾittĕkem — therefore I will still bring a lawsuit against you. The legal term rîḇ (contend, bring charges) is now explicit; YHWH is both plaintiff and judge. Wĕʾet-bĕnê ḇĕnêkem ʾāriḇ — and against your grandchildren's children I will bring a lawsuit: the covenant breach has multigenerational consequences (cf. Exod 20:5, visiting the iniquity to the third and fourth generation).
10: The proof from comparative religion: cross (ʿiḇrû) to Cyprus (western coast of the Mediterranean) and look; send to Kedar (Arabian desert nomads to the east) — the geographic extremes of the known world. Hahĕhēlîpû gôy ʾĕlōhîm — has a nation exchanged its gods? Rhetorically, the answer is no: even pagans are loyal to their (non-existent) gods. Israel's apostasy is uniquely irrational — they have traded a real God for non-gods.
11: Hēmîrû kĕḇôdô bĕlōʾ yôʿîl — they exchanged their glory for what profits nothing. Kāḇôd (glory) is what Israel traded away — the word refers to YHWH himself (cf. Ps 106:20, "they exchanged their glory for the image of a bull that eats grass"). The exchange is one of infinite loss: the divine glory for lōʾ yôʿîl (what does not profit/avail) — a practical description of idols: they cannot help.
12: Šōmmû šāmayim ʿal-zōʾt — be appalled, O heavens, at this. The heavens are called as witnesses to the covenant lawsuit (cf. Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2), and now as a horrified audience. Šāmam (be appalled/desolate) is the word used for devastated land — the heavens are called to react with the same stunned horror as if they had seen devastation. Śaʿărû ḥorĕḇû meʾōd — shudder and become very dry/desolate: the verbs escalate from shock to structural paralysis.
13: The double indictment: ʿāzāḇûnî mĕqôr mayim ḥayyîm — they abandoned me, the spring of living/flowing water. Māqôr is a natural spring (not a cistern), self-replenishing, inexhaustible. Mayim ḥayyîm — living water, meaning flowing/fresh water as opposed to stagnant; the same phrase Jesus uses in John 4:10-11 and 7:37-38, applying it to himself. The second charge: laḥṣōḇ lāhem bōrôt bōrôt nišbārîm — they hewn out for themselves cisterns, broken cisterns. The cistern (bôr) is an artificial pit lined with plaster to catch and hold rain — wholly dependent on human construction; when cracked (nišbārîm), it holds nothing. The contrast captures the theological irony: abandoning the inexhaustible self-replenishing source for a human construction that fails.
14: The rhetorical question: hăʿeḇed Yiśrāʾēl ʾim-yĕlîd-bayt hûʾ — is Israel a slave? is he a house-born servant? The yĕlîd-bayt (born in the house) is the lowest-status slave, born into slavery with no prior freedom. The question implies: if Israel is not a slave, why is he being treated as plunder? The political disasters (Assyrian and Babylonian aggression) are traced to the spiritual apostasy of ch2's charges.
15: ʿĀlāyw yišʾăgû kĕpîrîm — young lions roar over him. The lions (kĕpîrîm, young adult lions in their prime) are likely a metaphor for foreign powers, though unnamed. Wayyitrû ʾarṣô — they made his land a waste (šămmâ), cities burned, without inhabitants — the past tenses may anticipate the coming judgment or describe the Assyrian devastation of the northern kingdom as a type.
16: Gam-bĕnê-Nōp wĕTahpanḥēs — the people of Memphis (Noph) and Tahpanhes (in the Nile delta). These Egyptian cities represent the Egyptian alliance Judah was seeking as protection against Babylon — the very alliance YHWH forbids throughout Jeremiah. Yirʿûk qodqōd — they have shaved the crown of your head: a sign of subjugation and humiliation, stripping the dignity of the vassal. The irony is that Egypt, sought as a protector, treats Judah as conquered territory.
17: Hălōʾ-zōʾt taʿăśeh-lāk ʿāzĕḇēk ʾet-YHWH — is this not brought on yourself by abandoning YHWH your God? The reflexive causation is explicit: the disasters are self-inflicted through covenant abandonment. The phrase bĕʿēt môlîḵēk badderek — when he led you on the way — invokes the wilderness leading as the context of YHWH's provision that was then abandoned.
18: The oracle continues with a polemic against the foreign-alliance strategy: mah-lāk lĕdereḵ Miṣrayim — what is it to you on the Egyptian road? lištoṯ mê šîḥôr — to drink the waters of the Nile (šîḥôr = the dark/black waters of the Nile delta)? The metaphor of drinking foreign waters stands against the living spring of v13 — seeking political security from Egypt is substituting Nile water for YHWH's spring. Similarly for Assyria's Euphrates (Nahar).
19: Tĕyassĕrēk rāʿātēk — your own wickedness will discipline you. The verb yāsar (discipline, instruct through consequence) is the pedagogical term of Proverbs and Deuteronomy; the consequences of apostasy are built into the covenant structure — they are not arbitrary but inherent correctives. Wĕtôkĕḥēk mĕšuḇōtāyik — and your turning-away will rebuke you: mĕšuḇôt (apostasies, lit. turnings-away) is from šûḇ, the same root as repentance — the apostasies that turned Israel away will now turn back on them as rebuke.
20: Kî mēʿôlām šāḇarti ʿullēk pittaḥtî môsĕrōtāyik — for long ago I broke your yoke and tore off your chains. The Exodus is again invoked as the act of liberation; the yoke (ʿōl) and bonds (môsērôt) of Egyptian slavery were shattered by YHWH. The response that should have been gratitude and service was defiance: wattōʾmrî lōʾ ʾeʿăḇōd — you said, I will not serve! (ʿāḇad can mean serve God or serve as a slave — the refusal to serve YHWH is ironically the act of a freed slave refusing to serve her liberator). ʿal kol-gib‛â gĕḇōhâ (on every high hill) and taḥat kol-ʿēṣ raʿănān (under every green tree) — the standard Deuteronomic formula for Canaanite high-place worship (Deut 12:2).
21: Wĕʾānōkî nĕṭaʿtîkā śōrēq kullōh zeraʿ ʾĕmet — I planted you as a choice vine, wholly pure/true seed. Śōrēq is a specific variety of the finest grape vine (Gen 49:11; Isa 5:2; it denotes quality cultivation). Zeraʿ ʾĕmet — true/genuine seed — stresses the original quality of what was planted. Wĕʾêk nĕhăpaḵtā-lî sûrê haggepen nōkrîyâ — how have you turned against me into the degenerate shoots of a foreign/alien vine? The transformation is complete reversal: the finest cultivated vine has degenerated into a wild foreign stock — not by natural process but by willful apostasy.
22: Kî ʾim-tĕkabbĕsî baneṯer — even if you wash with lye (neṯer, a natural sodium carbonate used as soap/bleach). Wĕtarbi-lāk bōrît — and use much scouring-soap (bōrît). Despite the most thorough cleansing possible with available materials, nikṯām ʿăwōnēk lĕpānay — the stain of your iniquity is before me. The metaphor draws from laundry practice: some stains cannot be removed regardless of the cleaner used. Guilt before YHWH is not the superficial kind that soap can treat.
23: Êk tōʾmrî lōʾ niṭmēʾtî — how can you say, I am not defiled? The rhetorical challenge invites self-examination. Rĕʾî darkēk bagayʾ — look at your tracks in the valley (the valleys near Jerusalem were sites of Baal worship, including the Valley of Hinnom). The animal metaphors follow: bikriyâ qallâ mĕšarreḵet dĕrāḵêhā — a swift young camel (bikriyâ, a female camel in heat) running in every direction; the animal's frantic, aimless galloping captures Israel's undirected, compulsive pursuit of idols.
24: Pereʾ limmûd midbār — a wild donkey accustomed to the wilderness (midbār). The pereʾ is the wild ass — untameable, driven by instinct. Bĕʾawwat napšāh šāʾăpâ rûaḥ — in her craving she sniffs the wind (tracking the scent of a male). The sexual drive of the animal in heat becomes the metaphor for Israel's idolatrous compulsion — the seeking of idols is as instinctual and uncontrollable as animal mating drive. Mĕḇaqqĕšêhā lōʾ-yiyʿāpû — those who look for her need not tire themselves; they will find her in her season — idolatry comes naturally and predictably, not requiring effort to entice.
25: Minĕʿî raglek miḥāyēp ûgĕrōnēk miṣṣāmāʾ — keep your foot from going bare and your throat from thirst (YHWH's futile warning). The imagery of the barefoot wanderer and the thirsty mouth suggests the destitution that the idolatrous pursuit leads to — there is an ironic echo with the wilderness wandering that YHWH provided for (Deut 8:4, their sandals did not wear out). But the response: wattōʾmrî nôʾāš lōʾ kî ʾāhaḇtî zārîm wĕʾaḥărêhem ʾēlēk — you said, It is hopeless! For I love other gods and after them I will go. The word nôʾāš (hopeless) signifies a surrender of will — not just mistake but deliberate choice confirmed by love-language (ʾāhaḇtî = I love).
26: Kebōšet haggannāḇ kî yimmāṣēʾ — as a thief is shamed when caught. The shame-metaphor invokes the social visibility of the judgment. The comprehensive list — kings, officials, priests, prophets — mirrors the leadership list of 2:8; the entire social order from top to bottom will be exposed.
27: ʾōmĕrîm lāʿēṣ ʾāḇî ʾāttâ — saying to a tree, You are my father. The sacred tree in Canaanite religion was the Asherah pole (ʾăšērâ), representing the goddess consort of Baal; calling the tree "father" is the gender inversion of the expected "mother" — the text may reflect confusion of Baalism's gender associations. Wĕlāʾeḇen ʾat yĕladtānî — and to a stone, You gave me birth. The standing stone (maṣṣēḇâ) was Baal's emblem. The irony: they turn their backs (ʾōrep) to YHWH but their faces to creation objects — yet in crisis they cry qûmâ wĕhôšîʿēnû (arise and save us).
28: The taunting challenge: wĕʾayyēh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾăšer ʿāśîtā lāk — and where are the gods you made for yourself? ʾĂšer ʿāśîtā (that you made) is the devastating qualifier — these are manufactured deities. Yāqûmû wĕyôšîʿûkā — let them arise if they can save you. The irony is structural: gods that must be told to "arise" (as if sleeping or absent) are not gods who can save. The proliferation of deities is then mocked: kî mispar ʿārêkā hāyû ʾĕlōhêkā — for as many as your cities are your gods, O Judah.
29: Lāmmâ tārîḇû ʾēlay — why do you bring a lawsuit against me? The same rîḇ (lawsuit) vocabulary continues; YHWH turns the legal challenge back: you are all apostates (kullĕkem pĕšaʿtem bî — you have all transgressed against me), so any complaint against YHWH is without standing.
30: Lašwāʾ hikkêtî ʾet-bĕnêkem — in vain I struck your children; they accepted no correction (mûsār). The divine discipline intended to produce repentance has failed; the result is no change. ʾAkĕlâ ḥarḇĕkem nĕḇîʾêkem kĕʾaryēh mašḥît — your sword has devoured your prophets like a destroying lion: the people themselves killed the prophets sent to correct them (cf. Matt 23:37).
31: Haddôr ʾattem rĕʾû dĕḇar-YHWH — O generation, give heed to the word of YHWH. The word dôr (generation) is a collective indictment — the entire contemporary generation is complicit. Hămidbār hāyîtî lĕYiśrāʾēl — was I a wilderness to Israel? YHWH's self-defense: was I the source of want, not of provision? The wilderness was where YHWH provided, not withheld — yet Israel acts as if YHWH were the problem. Wattōʾmĕrû rādnû lōʾ-nāḇôʾ ʿôd ʾēlêkā — and you said, We are free/rulers; we will come to you no more: rādnû can mean "we have mastery/rule" (from rādâ) or "we will wander" — the former more likely, a declaration of self-sufficiency and rejection of the relationship.
32: Hattiškah bĕtûlâ ʿădyāhā — can a young woman forget her jewelry? Kallâ qiššurêhā — a bride her wedding attire? The absurdity of the comparison makes the indictment: the two things most precious to a young woman (jewelry and bridal dress) are never forgotten — yet wĕʿammî šĕkĕḥûnî yāmîm ʾên mispar — my people have forgotten me for more days than can be counted. The duration (countless days) compounds the absurdity: not a momentary forgetting but a sustained, deliberate one.
33: Mah-tîṭĕḇî darkēk lĕḇaqqēš ʾahăḇâ — how skilled you are at pursuing love. The irony is that Israel has become an expert in the pursuit of spiritual adultery — gam ʾet-hārāʿôt limmadtî ʾet-dĕrākāyik — you have even taught the wicked your ways. The apostasy has become instructional; Israel has become the teacher of wickedness rather than the teacher of Torah.
34: Gam bĕkapāyik nimṣĕʾû dam nĕqiyyîm ʾeḇyônîm — on your garments is found the blood of innocent poor people. The social justice charge enters the covenant lawsuit: the same indictment will dominate later oracles. Lōʾ-bammakterent mĕṣāʾtîm — you did not find them breaking in (i.e., you cannot invoke the law of self-defense, Exod 22:2). The blood is on their garments without legal justification.
35: Wĕʾattem ʾămartem kî niqêtî — yet you say, I am innocent! The denial is total — niqāh (be clean, acquitted) is the legal term for acquittal. The contradiction between the evidence (v34: blood on their garments) and the claim (innocence) is the judicial heart of the case. Hinĕnî nišpāṭ ʾōtāk ʿal-ʾōmrēk lōʾ ḥāṭāʾtî — I will bring judgment because you say, I have not sinned: the denial itself becomes the cause of judgment — it forecloses repentance.
36: Mah-tĕzilî meʾōd lĕšannôt ʾet-darkēk — why do you rush about so much, changing course? The image is of anxious, indecisive policy: one foreign alliance abandoned for another. Gam-mimmitsrayim tēḇōšî kăʾăšer-bōšt mēʾaššûr — you will be ashamed by Egypt just as you were ashamed by Assyria: historical pattern — the Assyrian alliance (c. 700 BCE) failed; the Egyptian alliance (ongoing) will similarly fail. Foreign political security is a pattern of repeated disappointment.
37: Gam mēʾēt zeh tēṣĕʾî wĕyāḏêkî ʿal-rōʾšēk — from Egypt too you will leave with your hands on your head: the posture of a captive or defeated person (hands on head, a universal gesture of defeat and despair; cf. 2 Sam 13:19). Kî māʾas YHWH bemiḇṭaḥāyik wĕlōʾ tatsliḥî lāhem — for YHWH has rejected the ones you are trusting in. The verb māʾas (reject, despise) is the language of divine rejection of what is counted on for security — the foreign alliances are rejected at the source before they fail.
